content.text: model runs multiple coroutines concurrently, and when several of them fail at the same time, you need a clean way to group those failures and handle each type selectively. Python 3.11 introduced

syntax specifically to solve this problem. They form a single cohesive feature that lets you express "here are multiple failures that happened together" and then surgically route each type to a dedicated handler.

is a built-in exception type that wraps a collection of other exceptions into one object. It represents the concept of multiple things going wrong simultaneously — something that could not be expressed cleanly with a single traditional exception.
is its parent class, used when the grouped exceptions include
clause (note the asterisk) is a new kind of exception handler that specifically works with
clause — which catches one exception and stops looking —
inspects the group, extracts all exceptions of the matching type, and lets unmatched exceptions automatically propagate as a new, smaller group.
Before Python 3.11, if you ran multiple coroutines under

and several of them raised exceptions, you faced an ugly choice: either catch only the first failure and lose the rest, or build fragile workarounds storing errors in a list. The language had no native way to say "three things went wrong at once, here they all are."
containing all the failures when the group exits, which means every failure is visible and attributable.
completes that story by giving you a structured way to respond to those failures.
Think of a regular exception as a single envelope arriving in the mail. You open it, read the message, and deal with it. An
is a bundle of envelopes delivered together because they all arrived in the same batch. The

clause is like a sorting machine that opens the bundle, pulls out all envelopes of one kind (say, billing notices), lets you deal with those, and sends the remaining envelopes further up the chain. You can have multiple

clauses, each pulling out a different kind. Whatever is left at the end gets re-bundled and re-delivered upward automatically — you never have to worry about accidentally swallowing an exception type you didn't handle.
takes a string message and a sequence of exception instances:

eg = ExceptionGroup("network failures", [
    ConnectionError("host A unreachable"),
    TimeoutError("host B timed out"),
    ConnectionError("host C refused"),
])
The contained exceptions are available as

ExceptionGroup("mixed", [ValueError("oops"), KeyboardInterrupt()])
# → BaseExceptionGroup, not ExceptionGroup, because of the KeyboardInterrupt
trees. When Python splits a group looking for matching exceptions, it descends into nested sub-groups recursively. You do not need to flatten anything manually.
The diagram above illustrates the full lifecycle: tasks inside a
fail, the context manager collects their exceptions into a single
splits it, matched exceptions go to their handler, unmatched exceptions are automatically re-raised to the outer scope.
and want fine-grained control over each failure type
You are running multiple independent operations (HTTP requests, database queries, file processing) and need to handle partial failures without aborting everything
You are writing a library that fans out work and wants to report all failures to the caller rather than just the first
You are building retry or circuit-breaker logic where different error types warrant different responses
Sequential code where only one thing can fail at a time — a regular
Simple aggregation of results where you are fine with all-or-nothing semantics —
Code that needs to run on Python versions below 3.11
Step 1 — Use TaskGroup to collect concurrent failures
in async code. When any task in the group raises an exception, the group cancels the remaining tasks and collects all raised exceptions into an

import asyncio

async def risky_task(name: str, should_fail: bool):
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
    if should_fail:
        raise ValueError(f"{name} had bad input")

async def main():
    try:
        async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
            tg.create_task(risky_task("A", should_fail=False))
            tg.create_task(risky_task("B", should_fail=True))
            tg.create_task(risky_task("C", should_fail=True))
    except* ValueError as eg:
        print(f"Caught {len(eg.exceptions)} ValueError(s):")
        for exc in eg.exceptions:
            print(f"  - {exc}")

asyncio.run(main())

Caught 2 ValueError(s):
  - B had bad input
  - C had bad input
Step 2 — Handle multiple exception types with multiple except
clause is evaluated independently against the full group. They do not chain like
— all of them can run if the group contains multiple types.
Step 3 — Construct ExceptionGroup manually when fanning out non-task work
yourself when you collect failures from any concurrent or batched operation — not just
Step 4 — Use subgroup() and split() for programmatic inspection
When you need to inspect or route exceptions without
methods give you the same splitting logic as a programmatic API.

eg = ExceptionGroup("errors", [
    ValueError("bad value"),
    TypeError("wrong type"),
    ValueError("another bad value"),
    KeyError("missing key"),
])

value_errors, rest = eg.split(ValueError)
print(value_errors)  # ExceptionGroup('errors', [ValueError('bad value'), ValueError('another bad value')])
print(rest)           # ExceptionGroup('errors', [TypeError('wrong type'), KeyError('missing key')])

# subgroup returns None if nothing matches
http_errors = eg.subgroup(lambda e: hasattr(e, 'status_code'))
print(http_errors)   # None
Step 5 — Nest ExceptionGroups for hierarchical reporting
When you have multiple parallel sub-batches each producing their own failures, you can nest

Forgetting that unmatched exceptions propagate automatically.
A common misconception is that if your
clause does not re-raise, the unmatched exceptions are lost. They are not — Python handles re-raising unmatched exceptions automatically. You should never write
clause unless you want to re-raise the matched exceptions too.
block. Choose one or the other. If you need to catch both grouped and ungrouped exceptions in the same code, use nested

except ValueError:

except* TypeError as eg:  # SyntaxError: cannot mix 'except' and 'except*'
    ...
Suppressing exceptions by not re-raising inside except

handler runs but does not raise anything, the matched exceptions are considered handled and do not propagate. This is usually intentional, but accidentally writing an empty handler body is a silent swallower.
If only one thing can go wrong at a time (sequential code), wrapping it in an
signals concurrent failure — using it in sequential contexts confuses readers and gives no benefit.

When the number of member exceptions matters to your handler logic, always use
rather than assuming a count. Under high concurrency, the number of simultaneous failures can vary.
handlers focused. Each clause should handle one logical category of failure. If you find yourself checking the type of individual exceptions inside a handler, you probably need another
for complex routing logic that is easier to express programmatically than as
clauses — for example, when the routing condition involves exception attributes rather than just type.
is a pure Python object with O(n) construction where n is the number of grouped exceptions. The
operations are also O(n) because they must inspect every member. For the typical case of a few dozen concurrent tasks, this is completely negligible.
For very large fan-outs (hundreds or thousands of parallel tasks), the

can accumulate many exceptions. This has no impact on throughput during the success path, but the error path may allocate a non-trivial number of exception objects simultaneously. In practice this is bounded by the fan-out width, which is typically limited by connection pool sizes and semaphores rather than exception handling overhead.
There is no performance concern with using
in the success path — when no exception is raised, the clause is never evaluated.

remains useful when you need the results and exceptions together in a single list for further processing.
is superior when you want structured control flow, automatic task cancellation on failure, and per-type routing without manual inspection loops.
prevents the silent exception swallowing that plagued pre-3.11 async error handling. When any
clause runs, all exceptions not claimed by a handler are automatically re-raised — you cannot accidentally suppress them by forgetting a
handlers, preserving the language's standard ability to interrupt running programs. A

with all collected failures rather than raising only the first. Callers can always inspect
if they care only about the first failure, but they cannot recover information that was never reported. Transparent failure reporting is a reliability property, not just a convenience.
The automatic re-raise of unmatched exceptions means that adding new
clauses over time is safe: existing handlers cannot accidentally claim newly introduced exception types just because they were not anticipated when the handler was written.
is a built-in exception type that holds multiple exception instances; it represents parallel failures as a first-class concept.
